title: Rogue waves from noise-induced modulational instability of a plane wave
abstract: In the framework of the one-dimensional nonlinear Schrodinger equation (1D-NLSE) of the focusing type, the present paper studies numerically rogue waves (RWs) that emerge in the nonlinear stage of noise-induced modulational instability of a plane wave. For a large ensemble of simulations, all sufficiently large local maximums of the wavefield (i.e., RWs) are systematically collected and analyzed. It is shown that the frequency of occurrence of such maximums begins to increase from zero at the time, when the fourth-order moment of amplitude reaches its first (largest) local maximum, and then grows in an oscillatory manner approaching its asymptotic value at long time. Near the statistically stationary state, the 1D-NLSE generates a much larger number of RWs than a comparable linear system, but, in average, one RW affects a much smaller spatiotemporal area. The distribution of these RWs by maximum intensity represents an exponential-like function without noticeable changes in behavior, indicating a similar origin for RWs of significantly different amplitudes. The collected RWs are compared within a sufficiently large spatiotemporal window with nine exact solutions, of which two models reproduce RW dynamics much better than the others: a general collision of two solitons and a general collision of two Tajiri-Watanabe breathers; RWs are fitted with these collisions using pre-compiled databases of such solutions. The parameters of the two models turn out to be quite similar to the direct scattering transform spectrum of the whole wavefield, supporting a hypothesis that RWs may emerge due to synchronization of a few nonlinear modes (solitons or breathers) in presence of many other nonlinear modes of the wavefield.
summary: This paper numerically studies rogue wave formation in the 1D-NLSE under noise-induced modulational instability. It systematically analyzes these extreme events by comparing them against known exact solutions, finding that the dynamics are best described by the collision of a few nonlinear modes. The results suggest that RWs arise from the synchronization of these underlying nonlinear structures.
main_problem: The paper investigates the formation and characteristics of rogue waves (RWs) that emerge during the nonlinear stage of noise-induced modulational instability (MI) in a plane wave.
main_result: The study shows that RWs are significantly more numerous than in linear systems, and their dynamics are best approximated by the collision of two nonlinear modes, such as solitons or breathers.
method: The analysis combines numerical simulations of the 1D-NLSE with rigorous comparisons of the observed RWs against known exact solutions derived via the Inverse Scattering Transform (IST).
model_or_system: The primary model is the one-dimensional nonlinear Schrödinger equation (1D-NLSE) of the focusing type. The analysis compares RWs generated from noise-induced MI against various exact solutions, including multi-soliton and multi-breather collision models.
key_observables: Frequency of RW occurrence (monitored via the fourth-order moment $\kappa_4$), the intensity distribution of RWs, and the deviation measure $D_\Lambda$ used to quantify the fit quality between observed RWs and theoretical models.
important_parameters: The analysis depends on the initial noise spectrum (Gaussian vs. MI spectrum) and the parameters defining the best-fit models, such as soliton/breather eigenvalues ($\lambda_n$) and collision parameters.
main_assumptions: The core hypothesis tested is that RWs emerge due to the synchronization of only a few nonlinear modes (solitons or breathers) within the complex wavefield, although this is noted as potentially naive.
figures_summary: Figures compare the Probability Density Functions (PDFs) of intensity and phase slope across different spectral regimes (MI vs. Gaussian) and show nested rectangular regions used for calculating the deviation measure $D_\Lambda$ during model fitting.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical model (1D-NLSE) and the phenomenon (RWs from MI) to developing advanced mathematical tools (IST, dressing method) and finally comparing the numerically observed RWs against a comprehensive database of known exact solutions (soliton/breather collisions).
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to the study of open quantum systems and quantum optics because the 1D-NLSE is a canonical model for nonlinear wave propagation, and the study of extreme events like rogue waves provides insights into nonlinear energy localization and wave-wave interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (Color on-line) Rectangular regions in the (χ, τ)- variables, over which the deviations (41) between RWs and their fits with various exact solutions are calculated. Black lines show the nested rectangles Wm, m = 0, ..., 9, while red lines indicate the rectangular region Wf, in which the param- eters of the fits with exact solutions are calculated.

FIG. 2. (Color on-line) Evolution of the fourth-order mo- ment of amplitude κ4 = ⟨|ψ|4⟩/⟨|ψ|2⟩2 (black) and distribu- tion P(t0) of moments of time t0 when RWs reach their max- imum amplitude (green), scaled with a coefficient β = 307 for better visualization. The inset shows a zoomed-in beginning of the nonlinear stage of MI (the transient regime), with the red dashed lines indicating the moments of the first four local maximums of κ4 at t = 13.6, 17.7, 21.4 and 25, and the blue dashed lines – the moments of the first four local minimums at t = 15.7, 19.6, 23.3 and 26.8.

FIG. 4. (Color on-line) (a) Space-time evolution near the statistically stationary state of MI, t ∈[80, 200], from one re- alization of initial conditions (36). Color shows the intensity |ψ|2, while green dots indicate the positions of RW maxi- mums. (b) Random uniformly distributed positions (green dots) of the same number as the number of RWs detected in the experiment from panel (a). The color background is added for ease of comparison with panel (a) and corresponds to unit intensity.

FIG. 3. (Color on-line) (a) PDFs of intensity for the whole wavefield P(|ψ|2) (red) and for the maximums of collected RWs P(max |ψ|2) (blue), and (b) PDF of chirp ϕx = ∂arg ψ/∂x at the RW maximums, for RWs close to the statistically stationary state t ∈[80, 200] of MI. In panel (a), the black dash-dot line shows the exponential distribution exp(−|ψ|2), while the blue dashed line indicates exponential dependency ∝exp(−|ψ|2). In panel (b), the inset shows the same PDF in semi-logarithmic scale, while the black dashed line marks the Gaussian distribution of the same variance.

FIG. 5. (Color on-line) Ensemble-averaged PDFs of space- time distance to the nearest neighbor rnn for RW maxi- mums (thick black) and random uniformly distributed posi- tions (47) with density µ = 2.12×10−2 per unit area (dashed red). When calculating rnn, the space-time distance between two points is measured according to Eq. (46) and the pe- riodic boundary conditions are forced in the time interval t ∈[80, 200] to model points outside of this interval.
